serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of Kway Teow Kwah weighs approximately 350 g and consists of flat rice noodles served in a savoury thickened gravy with fish cake, bean sprouts, and garnishes.

nutrient_source.title: Kway Teow Soup / Kway Teow with Gravy — IMR Malaysia MyFCD and HPB Singapore Nutrient Composition Data
nutrient_source.organization: Institute for Medical Research (IMR) Malaysia — Malaysian Food Composition Database (MyFCD); Health Promotion Board Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single exact entry for 'Kway Teow Kwah' exists in MyFCD or HPB. Values are adapted from IMR MyFCD entry for 'Kway Teow Soup' (boiled flat rice noodles in broth) and cross-referenced with HPB Singapore data for similar noodle-in-gravy dishes. The gravy component (thickened with cornstarch, containing fish cake and bean sprouts) is consistent with standard Malaysian hawker preparation. Per-100g values reflect the composite dish as served.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Rice noodles (boiled, Asian-style flat rice noodles)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition, 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Kway Teow Kwah as a composite dish. GI of 53 is derived from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for boiled flat rice noodles (Asian-style), which is the primary glycemic driver of this dish. The thickened gravy and protein/fat components are expected to moderate the overall GI slightly. GI category is low (≤55) per Veritas threshold rules. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (53 × 43.75) / 100 ≈ 23.2, rounded to 23.1 (serving size 350g, carbohydrate_g per 100g = 12.5g, so 43.75g per serving).

hypertension.guidance: Kway Teow Kwah contains approximately 310 mg sodium per 100 g (approximately 1085 mg per standard 350 g bowl), which represents a significant sodium load; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size and request reduced-sodium gravy preparation where possible.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Refined carbohydrate content and glycemic load
type2_diabetes.guidance: The flat rice noodles in Kway Teow Kwah have a GI of approximately 53 (low category) but the glycemic load per standard bowl is approximately 23, which is moderate-to-high; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should monitor portion size and pair with additional protein or non-starchy vegetables to blunt postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The high sodium content (~1085 mg per bowl) and moderate saturated fat (~2.45 g per bowl) in Kway Teow Kwah are relevant for cardiovascular risk management; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit frequency of consumption and choose preparations using vegetable oil rather than lard.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Kway Teow Kwah (also spelled Kueh Teow Kuah or Kway Teow Kuah) is a Malaysian and Singaporean hawker noodle dish consisting of flat, wide rice noodles (kway teow) served in a savoury, thickened gravy rather than a clear broth. The dish is a variant of the broader kway teow family and is particularly popular in Malaysian hawker centres and coffee shops. The gravy is typically made from pork or chicken stock thickened with cornstarch, seasoned with soy sauce, garlic, and white pepper, and served over blanched flat rice noodles. Common accompaniments include sliced fish cake, bean sprouts, spring onion, and sometimes prawns or pork slices. The texture is soft and silky from the noodles, with a glossy, savoury, mildly starchy gravy. The flavour profile is umami-forward, mildly salty, and subtly sweet from the stock. Nutritionally, the dish is predominantly carbohydrate-based (approximately 12.5 g per 100 g) with moderate protein (3.2 g per 100 g) from fish cake and meat components, and relatively low fat (2.1 g per 100 g). Sodium content is notable at approximately 310 mg per 100 g, making it a moderate-to-high sodium dish per standard serving. The glycemic index of the rice noodle base is approximately 53 (low GI), though the glycemic load per bowl is moderate. Clinically, this dish warrants attention for individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, or cardiovascular disease due to its sodium and refined carbohydrate content. The dish is not suitable for halal diets when prepared with pork-based stock or lard, though halal variants using chicken stock are widely available.

confidence_reason: No single exact entry for Kway Teow Kwah exists in IMR MyFCD or HPB Singapore databases. Nutrient values are adapted from the closest available entries for kway teow soup and similar noodle-in-gravy dishes from approved regional sources. GI is estimated from the analogous boiled flat rice noodle entry in the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than high due to the adapted rather than exact source match.
notes: Vitamin A (vitamin_a_mcg_rae) is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source entries for this dish. Vitamin B6 (vitamin_b6_mg) is -1 as it is not reported in the source database for this dish. Vitamin B12 (vitamin_b12_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the source database for this dish. Folate (folate_mcg_dfe) is -1 as it is not reported in the source database for this dish. Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the source database for this dish. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is -1 as it is not reported in the source database for this dish. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the source database for this dish. Trans fat (trans_fat_g) is 0.0 as this is a traditionally prepared hawker dish with no industrial partially hydrogenated oils; confirmed negligible per source analogy. The cuisine tag 'Dosa' in the input record appears to be a data error from the field survey seed record and has been disregarded. Halal status is marked false as the traditional preparation uses pork-based stock and lard; halal variants exist but cannot be assumed. The GI category is low (GI = 53, which is ≤ 55 per Veritas threshold rules).
